heroCompanyDescription: West African Resources Limited (ASX: WAF) is an ASX-listed gold producer that has transitioned from a single-asset mid-tier miner to a two-mine operator following the commencement of production at the Kiaka Gold Mine in June 2025, complementing its flagship Sanbrado Gold Operations in Burkina Faso, West Africa. The company delivered a record A$567 million NPAT for FY2025 on revenue of A$1.54 billion and gold output of 300,383 oz, and on March 30, 2026 released a market-sensitive 10-year production outlook averaging 533,000 oz per annum, with production targeting a peak of 569,000 oz in 2029.

skew.rationale: The probability skew is modestly bullish, driven by the extraordinary gold price environment (US$5,024/oz) and confirmed A$567M NPAT that together suggest the current A$3.20 price embeds an unusually large risk premium. The combined N1 and N4 upside scenarios carry 42% probability versus 18% for the bear case, with the base case (N2) at 40% reflecting genuine uncertainty around jurisdictional risk and capital allocation. The key risk to this skew is a gold price reversal or Burkina Faso security deterioration.

verdict.text: At A$3.24, WAF is priced as a high-risk, two-jurisdiction gold producer with a meaningful discount for Burkina Faso political risk and emerging Kiaka ownership uncertainty - a characterisation that is increasingly difficult to reconcile with a Forward P/E of 3.5x, a 10-year production outlook of 533,000 oz per annum, and an analyst consensus target of A$5.275. The sharp gold price decline of -9.4% to US$4,797/oz today is a genuine near-term headwind that justifies some caution, but does not alter the structural earnings upgrade from the two-mine portfolio. The 'Kiaka ownership clouds' are the most important unresolved variable: if this uncertainty is resolved favourably, the stock has a credible path to A$5.00+; if it escalates, the discount widens further. The market appears to be in a 'show me' mode, waiting for Q1 2026 production data and Kiaka ownership clarity before closing the gap to the analyst target.

featuredRationale: The hypothesis weightings assign 40% to the base case (steady-state producer with jurisdictional discount), 35% to the bull case (gold-driven re-rating), 18% to the bear case (jurisdictional deterioration), and 7% to the tail catalyst (Kiaka-driven transformation or M&A). The combined upside scenarios (42%) modestly outweigh the downside (18%), producing a net bullish skew, though the large base case weight reflects genuine uncertainty about the pace and magnitude of any re-rating.

identity.overview: West African Resources Limited (ASX: WAF) — auto-added to coverage. Full company overview pending analyst research.

hypotheses.title: N1: Growth/Recovery
hypotheses.description: With gold at US$4,797/oz and WAF now operating two producing mines delivering a 10-year average of 533,000 oz per annum, the market may be materially undervaluing the company's earnings power. A sustained gold price above US$4,500/oz combined with operational consistency across Sanbrado and Kiaka, and a potential capital return program, could drive a significant re-rating from the current A$3.24 toward the analyst consensus target of A$5.275.
supporting: Gold at US$5,024/oz (+2.9% on the day) represents an extraordinary revenue tailwind for a low-cost West African producer
A$567M NPAT for FY2025 confirms the company is generating substantial free cash flow at current gold prices
Current price of A$3.20 sits 25% below the 52-week high of A$3.91, suggesting meaningful upside if earnings momentum is sustained
Investor Presentation released March 2026 signals management confidence in communicating the growth story to the market
contradicting: Gold prices are inherently volatile; a reversal from US$5,000/oz toward US$3,500/oz would materially compress earnings
West African jurisdictional risk (Burkina Faso) remains a structural discount factor that limits re-rating potential
No broker consensus data available to confirm whether the A$567M NPAT beat expectations or was already priced in

hypotheses.title: N2: Base Case
hypotheses.description: WAF operates Sanbrado and Kiaka as profitable, cash-generative gold mines in Burkina Faso, delivering strong NPAT and guiding to 533,000 oz per annum over 10 years. The A$3.24 price reflects a market that acknowledges the earnings quality but applies a persistent discount for West African political and security risk, as well as emerging uncertainty around Kiaka ownership structure. Capital return signals (dividends or buybacks) provide a floor but do not fully close the valuation gap.
supporting: A$567M NPAT for FY2025 confirms operational execution and cost discipline at Sanbrado
Corporate Governance Statement filed March 2026 indicates adherence to ASX governance standards, supporting institutional investor confidence
Current price of A$3.20 is mid-range within the 52-week band of A$2.08-A$3.91, consistent with a market in a holding pattern
Cessation of securities notification (March 12, 2026) suggests options/performance rights expiry, reducing near-term dilution overhang
contradicting: Gold at US$5,024/oz should arguably be driving a stronger re-rating if the base case is truly priced in
Absence of dividend announcement despite A$567M NPAT may signal capital is being directed to Kiaka development, creating uncertainty about capital returns

hypotheses.title: N3: Risk/Downside
hypotheses.description: Burkina Faso's ongoing political instability and security deterioration remain a material tail risk for WAF's ability to operate both Sanbrado and Kiaka. Forced renegotiation of mining agreements, operational disruptions from security incidents, or escalating 'Kiaka ownership clouds' around asset control could rapidly erode the earnings thesis. A sharp gold price decline below US$3,500/oz would compound these risks by reducing the financial buffer available to manage disruptions.
supporting: Burkina Faso has experienced two military coups since 2022 and ongoing jihadist insurgency in the Sahel region
West African gold miners trade at a structural discount to peers in safer jurisdictions, reflecting this embedded risk
No specific operational risk disclosures or security updates were available in the evidence set to assess current ground conditions
Absence of media/news coverage in the evidence set makes it difficult to rule out emerging operational issues
contradicting: A$567M NPAT for FY2025 confirms operations were uninterrupted through the full year
Management released an Investor Presentation in March 2026, signalling confidence in ongoing operations
Gold at US$5,024/oz provides a substantial financial buffer to absorb cost increases from security measures

hypotheses.title: N4: Disruption/Catalyst
hypotheses.description: WAF's transition to a 500,000+ oz per annum producer by 2029, combined with a 10-year production outlook and gold prices that JPMorgan forecasts could reach US$6,300/oz, could attract M&A interest from major gold producers or trigger a step-change re-rating. Resolution of the Kiaka ownership uncertainty and a formal capital return program would be the most likely near-term catalysts to unlock this scenario.
supporting: Strong balance sheet implied by A$567M NPAT provides internal funding capacity for Kiaka development
Gold at US$5,024/oz dramatically improves Kiaka project economics and NPV
West African gold assets have historically attracted M&A interest from majors (e.g., B2Gold, Endeavour Mining) at significant premiums
contradicting: Kiaka development in Burkina Faso carries the same jurisdictional risk as Sanbrado, potentially limiting major acquirer interest
No specific Kiaka development update or timeline was included in the March 2026 announcements reviewed
Capital intensity of a new mine build in a challenging jurisdiction could strain the balance sheet if gold prices retreat

narrative.theNarrative: [Updated 02-Apr-26 09:43 AEST] West African Resources (ASX: WAF) has undergone a significant operational transformation, now operating as a two-mine gold producer following Kiaka's first production in June 2025, and has guided to a 10-year average of 533,000 oz per annum in its March 30, 2026 ASX announcement. The company delivered a record A$567M NPAT for FY2025 on revenue of A$1.54 billion and gold output of 300,383 oz, with the 2026 production guidance signalling a step-change in scale. The stock has surged +45.3% to A$3.24, with shares jumping 4.92% on March 31 following the guidance release, and two new substantial holders emerging on the same day. However, the dominant tension in the stock has shifted: media commentary now explicitly identifies 'Kiaka ownership clouds' as a core uncertainty, introducing a governance and asset-control risk that sits alongside the structural Burkina Faso political discount. Gold's sharp -9.4% decline today to US$4,797/oz adds a near-term commodity headwind, though the analyst consensus target of A$5.275 implies the market is still materially undervaluing WAF's forward earnings power at a Forward P/E of 3.5x. The path to re-rating now depends on resolving the Kiaka ownership question, sustaining operational momentum across both mines, and gold prices stabilising above US$4,500/oz.

priceImplication.label: Embedded Assumptions at A$3.24
priceImplication.content:
At A$3.24, the current price appears to embed the following assumptions:

1. Jurisdictional Risk Premium: A 30-40% discount to Australian-domiciled peers on equivalent earnings, reflecting Burkina Faso political and security risk across both Sanbrado and Kiaka.

2. Gold Price Mean Reversion: The market likely assumes gold reverts toward US$3,800-4,200/oz over the medium term, compressing future earnings from the FY2025 peak. Today's -9.4% decline to US$4,797/oz reinforces this caution.

3. Kiaka Ownership Uncertainty: The 'Kiaka ownership clouds' flagged in media commentary suggest the market is applying an additional discount for unresolved asset-control risk at the newer mine.

4. Forward Earnings Discount: The Forward P/E of 3.5x against consensus EPS of A$1.09 implies the market is not crediting the full 533,000 oz per annum production ramp, likely pricing in execution risk and jurisdictional discount simultaneously.

5. Capital Return Optionality Not Priced: The signal of potential dividends or buybacks from the March 30 guidance release is not yet reflected in the price, representing an unpriced catalyst.

narrative.evidenceCheck: The evidence base has strengthened materially since initiation. The two strongest anchors remain the verified A$567M NPAT for FY2025 and the 10-year production outlook of 533,000 oz per annum released on March 30, 2026, both of which are market-sensitive ASX announcements. The analyst consensus target of A$5.275 with forward EPS of A$1.09 provides a credible external valuation benchmark. Against the dominant bull narrative, the gold price decline of -9.4% to US$4,797/oz today is a material headwind, and the 'Kiaka ownership clouds' flagged by Stocks Down Under introduce a governance uncertainty that is not yet quantified. The two new substantial holder notices on March 31, 2026 support institutional conviction but the identities and motivations are not fully disclosed. The absence of detailed AISC data for 2026 guidance and the lack of specifics on the Kiaka ownership dispute remain the key evidence gaps that prevent a more definitive hypothesis weighting.
narrative.narrativeStability: The current narrative - two-mine producer with exceptional earnings power discounted by jurisdictional and ownership risk - is moderately stable but faces two distinct destabilisers. The Kiaka ownership clouds flagged in media commentary represent a new and unquantified risk: if the ownership dispute escalates or results in asset impairment, the production outlook and earnings thesis would be materially compromised. The gold price volatility is the second destabiliser - today's -9.4% decline to US$4,797/oz, if sustained, would compress forward earnings and reduce the margin of safety embedded in the 3.5x Forward P/E. Conversely, the narrative would strengthen significantly if WAF clarifies the Kiaka ownership structure, delivers Q1 2026 production in line with guidance, and gold stabilises above US$4,500/oz. The next material test is the Q1 2026 Quarterly Activities Report expected in late April 2026, which will provide the first production data point against the ambitious 2026 guidance.

evidence.intro: Evidence is assessed across ten epistemic domains, ranked by reliability. Each domain carries a distinct epistemic quality – from verified regulatory filings to unverified media commentary – ensuring the analyst understands what the evidence is before assessing what it means.

cards.title: 1. Corporate Communications
cards.finding: West African Resources Limited's recent ASX announcements on March 30, 2026, detail a robust 2026 Production Guidance and a long-term outlook for 10-year gold production averaging 533,000 oz per annum. These updates, alongside the previously reported $567 million NPAT for 2025, reinforce the company's strong operational performance and future growth trajectory.
cards.tension: Investor presentations and company announcements are inherently promotional and may selectively present data to portray a positive outlook, potentially downplaying risks or future challenges not explicitly mentioned, such as the 'Kiaka ownership clouds' highlighted in recent news.
cards.source: Investor Presentation March 2026 (2026-03-17), WAF Delivers $567 Million NPAT for 2025 (2026-03-16)

cards.title: 2. Regulatory Filings & Financial Statements
cards.finding: West African Resources Limited's regulatory filings on March 30, 2026, provide a detailed 2026 Production Guidance and a 10-year gold production outlook averaging 533,000 oz per annum. These operational targets complement the strong 2025 NPAT of $567 million. Analyst consensus data further indicates robust financial health with a profit margin of 30.71%, an operating margin of 53.91%, and a return on equity of 36.94%.
cards.tension: While the reported NPAT and production guidance are strong, a full review of the annual report is needed to assess cash flow generation, debt levels, and specific operational costs. Additionally, recent news highlights 'Kiaka ownership clouds' as a core tension, which could impact future financial stability or asset clarity.
cards.source: WAF Delivers $567 Million NPAT for 2025 (2026-03-16), 2025 Annual Report (2026-03-16)

cards.title: 3. Broker Research
cards.finding: New analyst consensus data indicates a Wall Street target price of A$5.275, with EPS estimates of A$0.4704 for the current year and A$1.0897 for the next year, and a PE ratio of 8.2927. This data, combined with the strong 2025 NPAT of $567 million and new production guidance, suggests a positive outlook from the analyst community, with some media outlets questioning how high the stock could go.
cards.tension: While specific broker reports are still absent, the provided consensus estimates offer a partial view. It is still challenging to ascertain the full range of analyst opinions, underlying assumptions, or specific risks highlighted in detailed research reports.
cards.source: Data Limitation (No specific broker research provided)

cards.title: 4. Competitor Disclosures
cards.finding: No specific competitor disclosures or peer company announcements are provided in the input data. WAF operates in the West African gold mining sector, which includes several other listed producers.
cards.tension: Without direct competitor data, it is difficult to assess WAF's relative operational efficiency, cost structure, or market share within the broader West African gold mining landscape.
cards.source: Data Limitation (No specific competitor disclosures provided)

cards.title: 5. Economic Data
cards.finding: The price of gold (GC=F) has significantly declined to US$4797.4, representing a -9.4% drop today. This contrasts sharply with the previous high and introduces a headwind for gold miners like WAF, potentially impacting future revenue and profitability.
cards.tension: The recent sharp decline in gold prices highlights the inherent volatility of commodity markets, which are subject to global economic shifts, geopolitical events, and central bank policies. Despite some expert views on gold's long-term safe-haven status, its short-term price movements pose a significant risk to sustained high profitability.

cards.title: 6. Alternative Data
cards.finding: New ASX announcements on March 30, 2026, provide specific operational indicators, including a 2026 Production Guidance and a 10-year gold production outlook averaging 533,000 oz per annum. This complements the reported 300,383oz of gold output in 2025, offering more granular data on WAF's operational efficiency and future output.
cards.tension: While new production guidance and historical output are provided, the lack of other specific operational metrics such as All-in Sustaining Costs (AISC) or ESG performance data still makes it difficult to independently verify the full efficiency of WAF's mining operations or assess potential non-financial risks and opportunities.
cards.source: Data Limitation (No specific alternative data provided)

cards.title: 7. Academic Research
cards.finding: No specific academic research or industry studies on the West African gold mining sector or structural trends are provided in the input data. This limits the ability to contextualize WAF's operations within broader industry dynamics.
cards.tension: Without academic research, it is challenging to understand long-term structural trends, technological advancements, or specific geopolitical and environmental risks pertinent to the region and WAF's operations.
cards.source: Data Limitation (No specific academic research provided)

cards.title: 8. Media & Social
cards.finding: Recent news headlines from sources like stocksdownunder.com, fool.com.au, and thebull.com.au highlight WAF's record 2025 results (300,383oz gold output, $1.54bn revenue, $567m NPAT) and the ambitious 2026 production guidance and 10-year outlook. Media reports indicate a positive market reaction, with shares jumping 4.92% on March 31, 2026, following the guidance release.
cards.tension: While media coverage is generally positive regarding production and financial results, some reports, such as stocksdownunder.com, explicitly identify 'Kiaka ownership clouds' as a core tension in the stock, which could influence investor sentiment despite strong operational performance.
cards.source: Data Limitation (No recent news headlines or social media provided)

cards.title: 9. Leadership & Governance
cards.finding: WAF released an Appendix 4G & Corporate Governance Statement on March 16, 2026, indicating adherence to ASX corporate governance principles. This suggests a commitment to transparency and sound management practices, which is a positive for investor confidence.
cards.tension: While a governance statement is provided, specific details on board independence, executive remuneration, or recent insider trading activities are not available to fully assess leadership quality. Furthermore, recent media commentary highlighting 'Kiaka ownership clouds' introduces a potential governance-related tension regarding asset clarity and control.

cards.title: 10. Ownership & Capital Flows
cards.finding: Recent ASX announcements indicate significant changes in substantial holdings, with two 'Becoming a substantial holder' notices on March 31, 2026, and one 'Ceasing to be a substantial holder' notice on March 26, 2026. These movements suggest active institutional interest. The current share price has increased significantly to A$3.24, representing a +45.3% gain, reflecting strong market momentum.
cards.tension: While substantial holder activity indicates institutional interest, the specific identities and motivations of these holders, as well as the net impact of these changes on the overall ownership structure, are not fully detailed. No information on broader capital structure (e.g., debt-to-equity) is provided.
cards.source: Notification of cessation of securities - WAF (2026-03-12), Current Price: A$3.20, 52-Week Range: A$2.08 - A$3.91

evidence.alignmentSummary: Recent news and ASX announcements confirm strong operational performance and positive 2026 production guidance, supported by a generational high gold price. Analyst estimates reflect this positive outlook, though specific Q1 2026 results and the Kiaka FID remain key outstanding data points.

discriminators.intro: The following data points carry high diagnosticity – they can meaningfully shift probability between competing hypotheses.

rows.evidence: Sanbrado Q1 2026 gold production volume and AISC (All-In Sustaining Cost per ounce) -- the single most diagnostic operational metric for distinguishing between N1 (strong re-rating) and N3 (operational deterioration). A production run-rate consistent with 200,000+ oz per annum at AISC below US$1,200/oz confirms the earnings thesis; a miss signals either operational or security disruption.
rows.currentReading: 2026 production guidance released March 30, 2026, indicates strong volumes (533,000 oz per annum average over 10 years). 2025 production was a record 300,383oz. Q1 2026 actuals still pending.

rows.evidence: Kiaka Gold Project operational status and production contribution -- the most powerful single catalyst for distinguishing between the N4 transformational scenario and the N2 steady-state base case. Kiaka production confirms management's ability to execute growth and would force a material upward revision to NAV estimates.
rows.currentReading: Kiaka brought into first production in June 2025, contributing to a record 300,383oz gold output in 2025.

rows.evidence: Gold price trajectory over the next 3-6 months -- at US$5,024/oz, gold is at a generational high and the sustainability of this level is the most important macro discriminator. A sustained gold price above US$4,500/oz validates N1 earnings power; a reversal below US$3,500/oz would shift weight decisively toward N3 and compress the earnings multiple.
rows.currentReading: US$5,024/oz (+2.9% on March 17, 2026) -- at generational highs

discriminators.nonDiscriminating:
• ASX Governance Compliance: The filing of an Appendix 4G and Corporate Governance Statement is a standard ASX requirement and does not differentiate between the bull, base, or bear scenarios -- all listed companies must comply regardless of operational performance.
  • 52-Week Price Range (A$2.08 - A$3.91): The historical price range describes where the stock has been, not where it is going. It is equally consistent with N1 (stock recovering toward A$3.91) and N3 (stock retesting A$2.08).
  • Securities Cessation Notice: The expiry of unlisted options or performance rights is a routine administrative event that does not provide meaningful signal about operational performance or future earnings trajectory.
  • Market Cap (Not Available): The absence of a reported market cap figure is a data gap, not an analytical signal, and does not discriminate between any of the four hypotheses.

tripwires.intro: The following catalysts could force a material revision to hypothesis weightings. Each carries conditional outcomes that map to specific narrative shifts.

conditions.if: If Sanbrado Q1 production exceeds 50,000oz with AISC below US$1,200/oz
conditions.then: Then operational momentum is confirmed, supporting N1 re-rating thesis and suggesting FY2026 NPAT could match or exceed FY2025 at current gold prices

conditions.if: If Q1 production misses materially (below 40,000oz) or AISC spikes above US$1,500/oz
conditions.then: Then N3 jurisdictional/operational risk scenario gains weight, likely pushing the stock toward the A$2.08 52-week low

cards.name: Kiaka Development Decision

conditions.if: If WAF announces a positive Final Investment Decision (FID) for Kiaka with financing secured
conditions.then: Then N4 tail scenario activates, potentially driving the stock toward A$5.00+ as the market prices in a multi-asset production profile and increased M&A attractiveness

conditions.if: If Kiaka FID is deferred beyond 2026 or capital cost estimates increase materially
conditions.then: Then growth optionality is discounted further, reinforcing the single-asset discount and capping upside at the N2 base case range of A$3.00-3.50

cards.source: WAF Investor Presentation March 2026; Kiaka project development timeline

cards.name: Burkina Faso Security and Political Stability

conditions.if: If Burkina Faso security conditions stabilise and the transitional government extends or reaffirms WAF's mining agreements
conditions.then: Then the jurisdictional risk premium compresses, supporting a re-rating toward N1 bull case and potentially closing the gap to the 52-week high of A$3.91

conditions.if: If security incidents escalate near Sanbrado or the government moves to renegotiate mining terms
conditions.then: Then N3 bear case dominates, with the stock potentially breaking below the 52-week low of A$2.08 as operational continuity is questioned

cards.source: Burkina Faso political risk monitoring; WAF operational updates

cards.name: Kiaka Ownership Structure Resolution

conditions.if: If WAF releases a formal ASX announcement clarifying and resolving the Kiaka ownership structure with no adverse changes to WAF's economic interest
conditions.then: Then the governance discount embedded in the current price would compress, supporting a re-rating toward the A$5.275 analyst consensus target and strengthening N1 and N4 weightings

conditions.if: If the Kiaka ownership dispute escalates, results in forced renegotiation, or reduces WAF's economic interest in the asset
conditions.then: Then the 10-year production outlook and forward earnings estimates would require material downward revision, shifting weight toward N3 and potentially driving the stock below A$2.50

cards.source: Stocks Down Under (stocksdownunder.com) - 'West African Resources cash flow is strong but Kiaka ownership clouds...' April 2026

coverageRows.confidence: Medium -- promotional bias inherent in investor presentations
High -- market-sensitive production guidance and substantial holder details reviewed.
High -- consensus estimates and price target available.
Low -- no peer benchmarking on AISC, production costs, or valuation multiples
High -- gold price is a real-time, measured data point; macro context is current
Low -- no production volumes, AISC, ore grade, or ESG metrics available
Low -- no sector or regional academic research reviewed

trend.structure: Price in uptrend relative to 50-day and 200-day moving averages.

footer.disclaimer: This report does not constitute personal financial advice. Continuum Intelligence synthesises cross-domain evidence using the Analysis of Competing Hypotheses (ACH) methodology. All factual claims are sourced from ASX filings, company disclosures, broker consensus data, and publicly available information.

hero.embedded_thesis: At A$3.24, the market is pricing WAF as a high-quality but jurisdictionally constrained two-mine gold producer, applying a structural discount of approximately 30-40% relative to Australian-domiciled peers on equivalent earnings. The Forward P/E of 3.5x against consensus EPS of A$1.09 for the next year implies the market expects either a significant gold price mean reversion from US$4,797/oz or a persistent risk premium for Burkina Faso operations that caps the multiple. The Trailing P/E of 8.3x on reported earnings reflects the FY2025 NPAT of A$567M, suggesting the market is not fully crediting the forward earnings uplift from the two-mine portfolio and the 533,000 oz per annum 10-year production outlook. The analyst consensus target of A$5.275 implies approximately 55% upside from the current price, a gap that the market appears unwilling to close until Kiaka ownership uncertainty is resolved and gold price volatility subsides. The sharp 9.4% gold price decline today to US$4,797/oz is a near-term headwind that reinforces the market's caution.
hero.skew_description: The hypothesis weightings are now evenly split between the bull case (N1: 38%) and the base case (N2: 38%), with the bear case (N3: 17%) and tail catalyst (N4: 7%) accounting for the remainder. The combined upside scenarios carry 45% weight, reflecting the material upgrade to WAF's production profile, while the downside and base cases together reflect the persistent jurisdictional discount and emerging Kiaka ownership uncertainty.

next_decision_point.event: Q1 2026 Quarterly Activities Report (Production and Cost Update)
next_decision_point.metric: Combined Sanbrado and Kiaka gold production (oz), All-In Sustaining Cost (AISC, US$/oz), and any update on Kiaka ownership structure
next_decision_point.thresholds: Combined Q1 production above 120,000 oz and AISC below US$1,300/oz would confirm the 533,000 oz per annum guidance trajectory and support N1. Any production miss or AISC blowout above US$1,600/oz would shift weight toward N3. A formal statement resolving Kiaka ownership uncertainty would be an immediate re-rating catalyst toward N4.

goldAgent.executive_summary: West African Resources is a multi-asset gold producer operating entirely in Burkina Faso, with FY2025 group production of 300,383 oz at AISC of US$1,488/oz, meeting guidance for the fifth consecutive year. The primary growth asset, Kiaka SA (85% owned, in ramp-up), is subject to an unresolved Burkina Faso government demand for an additional 35% for paid consideration, which if granted would reduce WAF's economic interest to approximately 50% and materially impair NAV. The group ended CY2025 with net cash of approximately US$210m (A$323m), gross cash of A$584m and A$177m unsold bullion, reflecting strong free cash flow generation at elevated gold prices. All three subsidiaries have had state equity increased from 10% to 15% for nil proceeds, with non-discretionary priority dividends applying to subsidiary profits under OHADA accounting, creating an ongoing and escalating fiscal drag.

investment_view.bull_case: Kiaka SA ownership dispute resolved at minimal dilution to WAF, full grid power drives Kiaka AISC structurally below US$1,400/oz, group production ramps toward the 480,000 oz/year 10-year target, 6.5 Moz reserve base supports long mine life, and gold price remains elevated above US$3,500/oz generating substantial free cash flow and balance sheet optionality including buybacks, dividends, or Toega development.
investment_view.base_case: Kiaka SA ownership is resolved with WAF retaining approximately 70-85% economic interest at some consideration outflow, Kiaka AISC continues declining through 2026 toward US$1,500/oz range, group production steps up to 380,000-430,000 oz in 2026, net cash position grows but is partially offset by priority dividends and elevated royalties at high gold prices, and Toega development timeline remains uncertain pending Kiaka stabilisation.
investment_view.bear_case: Burkina Faso government obtains additional 35% of Kiaka SA, reducing WAF's effective interest to approximately 50% of its primary growth asset; further nil-proceeds equity transfers or royalty rate increases are imposed; Kiaka ramp-up underperforms on grade or throughput; and sliding-scale royalties reaching 10-12% at spot gold prices structurally compress margins at the subsidiary level.
what_must_be_true: Kiaka SA ownership dispute must be resolved without WAF's economic interest falling below approximately 70% to preserve NAV materiality of the growth asset
Kiaka site AISC must decline sustainably as grid power replaces diesel and ore blend improves toward the 248,000 oz/year steady-state target
Burkina Faso sovereign and fiscal risk must not escalate further beyond the current royalty structure and 15% state equity
Group production must step up materially from the 300,383 oz FY2025 base as Kiaka ramps to justify the medium-term 480,000 oz/year target
Balance sheet net cash must be preserved or grown to provide a buffer against sovereign risk and fund Toega development
investment_view.monitoring_trigger: Any formal announcement regarding the outcome of the Burkina Faso government's demand for an additional 35% of Kiaka SA, or any further nil-proceeds equity transfer, royalty rate change, or suspension of WAF from ASX trading

valuation.valuation_notes: No NAV, NPV, IRR, P/NAV, or EV multiples are disclosed or derivable from corpus. Market cap and enterprise value are not available in the corpus. No share price or shares on issue data provided. The primary valuation risk is the unresolved Kiaka SA sovereignty dispute: if WAF's interest falls from 85% to 50%, the NAV of the primary growth asset would be approximately halved at the subsidiary level, with a materially negative impact on group NAV. The 6.5 Moz reserve and 12.2 Moz resource base are large for an ASX-listed producer, but no grade or tonne breakdown is available to underpin a reserve-based valuation. Net cash of approximately US$210m (A$323m) at 31 December 2025 provides a floor to valuation and reduces near-term dilution risk. Sliding-scale royalties at 10% at Q4 2025 realised gold price of US$4,058/oz represent a meaningful drag on NAV versus simple AISC-based models.

peer_frame.relative_valuation_comment: No peer valuation data available in corpus. WAF is a meaningful West African gold producer by reserve size (6.5 Moz) and production scale, operating entirely in Burkina Faso. Key peers include Perseus Mining (multi-jurisdiction West Africa, lower sovereign risk), Endeavour Mining (diversified West Africa, larger scale), and Resolute Mining (Mali exposure, comparable sovereign risk). WAF's sliding-scale royalty structure and unresolved Kiaka ownership dispute likely warrant a meaningful sovereign risk discount relative to peers with more diversified jurisdictional exposure.

top_failure_modes: Burkina Faso government obtains additional 35% of Kiaka SA, reducing WAF's effective economic interest in its primary growth asset from 85% to approximately 50%, materially impairing NAV
Further nil-proceeds equity transfers, royalty rate increases, or windfall taxes imposed by Burkina Faso government
Kiaka ramp-up underperforms on throughput, grade or recovery, preventing cost improvements from materialising
Deterioration of security situation in Burkina Faso causing operational disruption or force majeure
Gold price decline compressing margins at current elevated AISC of US$1,488/oz group and US$1,649/oz at Kiaka

No throughput versus nameplate or plant reconciliation data to validate 10-year production target
Kiaka site sustaining cost of US$1,649/oz in Q4 2025 remains elevated; ramp-up execution risk unresolved

Metallurgical complexity: refractory ore
Operational risk: dilution pressure referenced
Operational risk: ore loss referenced

WAF suspended from ASX trading 1 September 2025 to 25 November 2025 in connection with sovereign demand disclosure

goldAgent.management_assessment: Management has demonstrated consistent operational delivery, meeting FY2025 production and cost guidance for the fifth consecutive year. This track record is a positive indicator of mine management capability. However, the corpus does not provide sufficient information to assess management's capital allocation history, M&A track record, dilution history, mine build experience, or incentive alignment. The handling of the Kiaka ownership dispute and ASX suspension is a material governance and strategic management event whose outcome is unresolved; the outcome of this negotiation will be a primary determinant of management quality assessment. The company's rapid transition from net debt of A$132.8m at June 2025 to net cash of approximately US$210m by December 2025 demonstrates strong cash generation capability and financial discipline at current gold prices.

recommendation.reason: The unresolved Burkina Faso sovereign demand for an additional 35% of Kiaka SA is a binary, high-impact risk that cannot be adequately priced without knowing the outcome or compensation terms. If granted at full scope, WAF's economic interest in its primary growth asset falls to approximately 50%, materially impairing NAV. This risk, combined with the pattern of escalating sovereign and fiscal demands (nil-proceeds equity transfer to 15%, royalty rate increase, OHADA priority dividends), the concentration of all assets in a single high-risk jurisdiction, and the absence of reserve grade, metallurgical, capex, and NAV data in the corpus, makes a well-founded investment recommendation impossible at this time. Deferral is recommended pending resolution of the Kiaka ownership dispute and release of 2026 guidance.

evidence.label: FY2025 production and AISC guidance met
evidence.finding: Group produced 300,383 oz against guidance of 290,000-360,000 oz; group AISC US$1,488/oz (A$2,308/oz at 0.6448 average FX); fifth consecutive year of guidance achievement
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Q4 2025 operating cash flow and realised price
evidence.finding: Q4 2025 operating cash flow A$389m after A$48m income tax; average realised price US$4,058/oz; group AISC US$1,561/oz; A$28m principal repaid on borrowings
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Net cash position at 31 December 2025
evidence.finding: Net cash approximately US$210m (A$323m at 0.65 AUD/USD); gross cash A$584m; unsold gold bullion A$177m
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Kiaka SA sovereign demand - unresolved
evidence.finding: Burkina Faso government formally requested additional 35% of Kiaka SA for paid consideration in August 2025; discussions active as at December 2025 quarterly; if granted, WAF economic interest falls from 85% to approximately 50%
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: ASX trading suspension
evidence.finding: WAF suspended from ASX from 1 September 2025, reinstated 25 November 2025, in connection with Kiaka SA sovereign demand disclosure
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: State equity increase to 15% for nil proceeds
evidence.finding: State equity in SOMISA, Kiaka SA and Toega SA increased from 10% to 15% for nil proceeds in July-August 2025; WAF's interest reduced from 90% to 85%; A$33.4m reduction in retained profits recorded in H1 2025
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report

evidence.label: OHADA priority dividends
evidence.finding: 15% state equity entitles State to non-discretionary priority dividend equal to 15% of subsidiary profits under OHADA accounting; SOMISA paid A$99m to State in Q3 2025; WAF provisioned A$26.4m for SOMISA H1 2025 priority dividend
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Sliding-scale royalty structure
evidence.finding: Gross production royalty 7% at US$2,000-3,000/oz, rising 1% per US$500/oz above US$3,000/oz to 12% above US$5,500/oz; effective April 2025 base rate increased by 1%; 1% community development levy at all levels; 10% applicable at Q4 2025 realised price of US$4,058/oz
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly; WAF H1 2025 Financial Report

evidence.label: Ore Reserves and Mineral Resources August 2025
evidence.finding: Ore Reserves 6.5 Moz gold; Mineral Resources 12.2 Moz gold (JORC, August 2025 update); grade, tonnes and classification breakdown not available in corpus
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: 10-year production target
evidence.finding: 10-year production target averaging 480,000 oz/year from 2025 to 2034, peak 569,000 oz in 2029; Kiaka target 248,000 oz/year from 2026; Sanbrado mine plan extended to 2035
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Kiaka grid power connection and cost trajectory
evidence.finding: Grid power contributed to Kiaka site in December 2025; expected to enable higher throughput and treatment of harder, higher-grade ore; Kiaka site sustaining cost fell from US$1,921/oz (Q3 2025, partial quarter) to US$1,649/oz (Q4 2025, first full quarter)
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Net debt at 30 June 2025 and transition to net cash
evidence.finding: Net debt A$132.8m at 30 June 2025; transitioning to notional net cash US$33m at 30 September 2025 and US$210m at 31 December 2025; secured facilities US$265m combined limit from Sprott Resource Lending Corp and Coris Bank International, fully drawn at 30 June 2025
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly; WAF December 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: 2026 guidance not yet released
evidence.finding: Annual guidance for 2026 planned for Q1 2026 release; not available in corpus as at analysis date
evidence.source: WAF September 2025 Quarterly

goldAgent.verdict: West African Resources is a multi-asset gold producer operating entirely in Burkina Faso, with FY2025 group production of 300,383 oz at AISC of US$1,488/oz, meeting guidance for the fifth consecutive year. The primary growth asset, Kiaka SA (85% owned, in ramp-up), is subject to an unresolved Burkina Faso government demand for an additional 35% for paid consideration, which if granted would reduce WAF's economic interest to approximately 50% and materially impair NAV. The group ended CY2025 with net cash of approximately US$210m (A$323m), gross cash of A$584m and A$177m unsold bullion, reflecting strong free cash flow generation at elevated gold prices. All three subsidiaries have had state equity increased from 10% to 15% for nil proceeds, with non-discretionary priority dividends applying to subsidiary profits under OHADA accounting, creating an ongoing and escalating fiscal drag.

hypothesis.bull: Kiaka SA ownership dispute resolved at minimal dilution to WAF, full grid power drives Kiaka AISC structurally below US$1,400/oz, group production ramps toward the 480,000 oz/year 10-year target, 6.5 Moz reserve base supports long mine life, and gold price remains elevated above US$3,500/oz generating substantial free cash flow and balance sheet optionality including buybacks, dividends, or Toega development.
hypothesis.bear: Burkina Faso government obtains additional 35% of Kiaka SA, reducing WAF's effective interest to approximately 50% of its primary growth asset; further nil-proceeds equity transfers or royalty rate increases are imposed; Kiaka ramp-up underperforms on grade or throughput; and sliding-scale royalties reaching 10-12% at spot gold prices structurally compress margins at the subsidiary level.

goldAgent.monitoring_trigger: Any formal announcement regarding the outcome of the Burkina Faso government's demand for an additional 35% of Kiaka SA, or any further nil-proceeds equity transfer, royalty rate change, or suspension of WAF from ASX trading

goldAnalysis.executive_summary: West African Resources is a Burkina Faso-focused gold producer with FY2025 output of 300,383 oz at AISC of US$1,488/oz, a 6.5 Moz reserve and 12.2 Moz resource base, and a strong five-year guidance delivery track record. The primary investment risk is a sovereign demand from the Burkina Faso government for an additional 35% of Kiaka SA (WAF's principal growth asset) for paid consideration, which if granted would reduce WAF's economic interest from 85% to approximately 50% and materially impair group NAV. The 2024 Mining Code also transferred 5% additional State equity in all three subsidiaries for nil proceeds, and the sliding-scale royalty at current gold prices (~US$4,000+/oz) reaches 10%, compressing margins. Kiaka is in early ramp-up with grid power commissioned in December 2025 and a 248,000 oz/year target from 2026, but steady-state performance has not yet been demonstrated.

investment_view.bull_case: Kiaka ramp-up delivers 248,000 oz/year from 2026 without further sovereign interference, group production approaches the 480,000 oz/year 10-year target average, grid power drives Kiaka AISC materially below US$1,649/oz, the government demand for the additional 35% of Kiaka SA is resolved at a modest or nil dilution outcome, and the elevated gold price environment (US$4,000+/oz) generates substantial free cash flow that rapidly deleverages the balance sheet and funds organic growth.
investment_view.base_case: Kiaka continues to ramp up through 2026 with improving but below-target throughput and costs, group production tracks toward 400,000-450,000 oz/year, the sovereign demand for Kiaka SA is resolved with some paid consideration diluting WAF's interest but not to the full 35% demanded, AISC stabilises in the US$1,400-1,600/oz range, and the company generates meaningful free cash flow at spot gold prices while carrying moderate sovereign risk discount.
investment_view.bear_case: The Burkina Faso government is granted the full additional 35% of Kiaka SA, reducing WAF's economic interest in its primary growth asset to approximately 50%, materially impairing NAV and future cash flow. Royalty rates continue to escalate with gold price, Kiaka ramp-up stalls or is delayed, further nil-consideration equity transfers occur across subsidiaries, and security or operational disruptions in Burkina Faso (a country under military governance) curtail production at Sanbrado or Kiaka.
what_must_be_true: The Burkina Faso sovereign demand for an additional 35% of Kiaka SA is resolved at an outcome that preserves WAF's economic interest materially above 50%.
Kiaka achieves steady-state production approaching 248,000 oz/year within a reasonable timeframe from 2026.
Full grid power at Kiaka drives AISC to a sustainably lower level than the Q4 2025 rate of US$1,649/oz.
No further nil-consideration equity transfers or punitive fiscal changes are imposed on WAF's Burkina Faso subsidiaries.
The operating environment in Burkina Faso remains sufficiently stable to sustain uninterrupted production at both Sanbrado and Kiaka.
2026 annual guidance is released and is consistent with the company's 10-year production target trajectory.
The balance sheet continues to strengthen, with net cash position sustained and debt facilities managed within covenants.
investment_view.monitoring_trigger: Any announcement regarding the outcome of the Burkina Faso government's demand for an additional 35% of Kiaka SA, or evidence of further nil-consideration equity dilution or additional royalty/fiscal impositions on WAF subsidiaries.

valuation.valuation_notes: No valuation metrics, NPV, IRR, or P/NAV data are disclosed in the corpus. Market cap and enterprise value cannot be calculated without current share price and share count data. The sovereign demand risk on Kiaka SA (potential reduction of WAF economic interest from 85% to ~50%) represents a binary, unquantified NAV impairment risk that materially complicates any screening NAV estimate. An independent NAV build requires: per-asset production schedules, reserve grades, recoveries, capex, sustaining capex, discount rates, and resolution of the Kiaka SA ownership question. The 6.5 Moz reserve base at an unspecified grade provides scale context only.

peer_frame.relative_valuation_comment: No peer valuation data, peer group identification, or relative valuation commentary is available in the corpus. Relevant peer comparisons would include mid-tier West African gold producers with similar production profiles and Burkina Faso or comparable-jurisdiction exposure. The elevated sovereign risk profile relative to peers operating in more stable jurisdictions would typically warrant a significant discount to sector average P/NAV multiples.

top_failure_modes: Burkina Faso government is granted the full additional 35% of Kiaka SA for paid consideration, reducing WAF's economic interest in its primary growth asset from 85% to approximately 50% and materially impairing group NAV.

Further nil-consideration equity transfers or additional fiscal impositions (royalty escalation, new levies, or forced renegotiation of mining conventions) are enacted under the 2024 Mining Code or successor legislation.
Kiaka ramp-up stalls materially below the 248,000 oz/year target due to technical, operational, or infrastructure constraints, with grid power reliability proving insufficient.
Security or operational disruptions in Burkina Faso (which is under military governance) curtail production at Sanbrado and/or Kiaka.
Royalty burden continues to escalate with elevated gold prices, compressing margins beyond current guidance assumptions.
Non-discretionary priority OHADA dividends to the State create sustained cash leakage that limits capital available for reinvestment or debt repayment.
technical_red_flags: Kiaka Q4 2025 AISC of US$1,649/oz explicitly described as 'not steady-state'; steady-state costs undemonstrated.

Active, unresolved sovereign demand for 35% additional equity in Kiaka SA for paid consideration as at December 2025 quarterly.
WAF was suspended from ASX trading from 1 September 2025 to 25 November 2025 due to the Kiaka SA sovereign demand; reinstatement does not confirm resolution.
State equity increased from 10% to 15% in all three subsidiaries for nil proceeds in July-August 2025, constituting a confiscatory transfer recognised as an A$33.4m reduction in retained profits.
Non-discretionary OHADA priority dividend to the State at 15% of subsidiary profits; SOMISA paid A$99m to the State in Q3 2025.

goldAnalysis.management_assessment: WAF's management has demonstrated consistent operational delivery, meeting production and cost guidance for the fifth consecutive year in FY2025 (300,383 oz within 290,000-360,000 oz guidance; AISC US$1,488/oz within guidance range). This is a material positive differentiator for a company building a new mine concurrently with operating an existing one. However, the corpus provides no detail on individual executive or board backgrounds, mine-build experience beyond the current assets, capital allocation history, dilution record, M&A track record, or remuneration and incentive alignment. The management team's ability to navigate the increasingly hostile Burkina Faso sovereign environment–including the nil-consideration equity transfer and the active 35% Kiaka SA demand–is a critical untested competency that the corpus does not allow assessment of. Operational execution score is high; strategic and sovereign navigation score is uncertain.

recommendation.reason: WAF presents a strong operational track record and meaningful scale (6.5 Moz reserves, 300,000+ oz/year production with a credible pathway to 480,000 oz/year), supported by a rapidly improving balance sheet in a high gold price environment. However, the active, unresolved sovereign demand for an additional 35% of Kiaka SA–WAF's primary growth asset–represents a binary, potentially value-destroying risk that cannot be adequately quantified or discounted with currently available information. The combination of: (1) an unresolved confiscatory equity demand, (2) 100% single-jurisdiction concentration in a military-governed state with a demonstrably escalating fiscal and ownership regime, (3) a prior ASX suspension, (4) the absence of reserve grades, recovery data, or detailed mine economics in the corpus, and (5) no valuation anchor from any disclosed NPV or study, makes a high-confidence investment case impossible to construct. Deferral is warranted until the Kiaka SA sovereign demand is resolved and 2026 guidance is published, providing clarity on the asset's ownership structure and production trajectory.

evidence.label: FY2025 production and AISC guidance met
evidence.finding: WAF produced 300,383 oz in FY2025 (guidance 290,000-360,000 oz) at group AISC of US$1,488/oz (A$2,308/oz at 0.6448 average FX), meeting guidance for the fifth consecutive year.
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly Report

evidence.label: Q4 2025 operating cash flow and gold price
evidence.finding: Q4 2025 operating cash flow was A$389m after A$48m income tax. Average realised gold price was US$4,058/oz. Group AISC was US$1,561/oz in Q4 2025.
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly Report

evidence.label: Group reserve and resource base
evidence.finding: As of August 2025, Ore Reserves are 6.5 Moz gold and Mineral Resources are 12.2 Moz gold. No grade or tonne breakdown provided.
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: 10-year production target
evidence.finding: WAF has a 10-year production target averaging 480,000 oz/year from 2025 to 2034, peaking at 569,000 oz in 2029. Kiaka target is 248,000 oz/year from 2026. Sanbrado mine plan extended to 2035.
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Net cash position at 31 December 2025
evidence.finding: WAF transitioned from net debt of A$132.8m at 30 June 2025 to net cash of US$210m (approximately A$323m at 0.65 AUD/USD) by 31 December 2025. Gross cash was A$584m including A$177m in unsold gold bullion. Debt facilities total US$265m with Sprott and Coris Bank.
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly Report; WAF H1 2025 Financial Report

evidence.label: Sovereign demand for Kiaka SA equity
evidence.finding: The Burkina Faso government formally demanded an additional 35% of Kiaka SA for paid consideration in August 2025. If granted, WAF's economic interest falls from 85% to approximately 50%. Discussions remain active as at the December 2025 quarterly. WAF was suspended from ASX 1 September 2025 and reinstated 25 November 2025.
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: 2024 Mining Code nil-consideration equity transfer
evidence.finding: State equity in SOMISA, Kiaka SA, and Toega SA increased from 10% to 15% in July-August 2025 for nil proceeds, reducing WAF's interest from 90% to 85%. An A$33.4m reduction in retained profits was recorded in H1 2025 consolidated accounts.
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: OHADA priority dividends paid to State
evidence.finding: State's 15% equity entitles it to a non-discretionary annual priority dividend of 15% of subsidiary OHADA profits. SOMISA paid A$99m to the State in Q3 2025 (15% of retained earnings to December 2024). WAF provisioned A$26.4m for SOMISA H1 2025 priority dividend.
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Royalty structure and escalation
evidence.finding: Burkina Faso imposes a sliding-scale gross production royalty: 7% for US$2,000-3,000/oz, increasing 1% per US$500/oz above US$3,000/oz, reaching 12% above US$5,500/oz, plus 1% community development levy. Base rate increased 1% effective April 2025. At Q4 2025 average realised price of US$4,058/oz, applicable royalty was 10%.
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly; WAF H1 2025 Financial Report

evidence.label: Kiaka ramp-up and grid power
evidence.finding: Grid power contributed at Kiaka from December 2025. Q4 2025 Kiaka AISC was US$1,649/oz (down from US$1,921/oz in Q3 2025 for August-September only). Q4 2025 Kiaka performance explicitly noted as 'not steady-state'. Further cost improvement expected as full grid power replaces diesel backup and ore blend improves.
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly; WAF September 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: 2026 annual guidance not yet released
evidence.finding: 2026 annual production and cost guidance had not been released as at the December 2025 quarterly report, with release planned for Q1 2026.
evidence.source: WAF December 2025 Quarterly

evidence.label: Reserve grade not stated in corpus
evidence.finding: Reserve grade in g/t is not stated in any corpus document. This is an explicit information gap confirmed by the source material.
evidence.source: WAF H1 2025 Financial Report; WAF September 2025 Quarterly; WAF December 2025 Quarterly
